ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT code identified for breaded fried chicken dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 150 g (one piece of crispy chicken cutlet), providing about 411 kcal, 30.3 g protein, 23.1 g fat, and 20.6 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, fried, breaded — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for breaded fried chicken at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (3.33 g/100 g), trans fat (0.11 g/100 g), and vitamin C (1.11 mg/100 g) were present in the input record at lower confidence (0.70); these have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, fried' (FDC ID 171477) and are retained as plausible. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy serves as the secondary approved source for cross-validation of these three fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index values for breaded and battered fried chicken products — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on refined wheat flour and cornstarch coating
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific Singapore-style crispy fried chicken. GI estimated at 70 (high) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for breaded and battered fried chicken products with refined wheat flour and cornstarch coatings, which consistently fall in the GI range of 65–75. Glycemic load per 100 g reference portion calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (70 × 13.7) / 100 = 9.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 948 mg per 100 g far exceeds the 600 mg/100 g threshold; a single 150 g serving delivers approximately 1,422 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the entire daily DASH diet sodium target of 1,500 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request reduced-sodium marinade, no added salt, and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as soy-based dipping sauces.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour and cornstarch breading produces a high estimated glycemic index (~70) with low dietary fibre (1.4 g/100 g), promoting rapid postprandial glucose elevation; deep-frying adds significant fat that may worsen insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit consumption of this dish; choose grilled or baked chicken without breading, pair with high-fibre sides, and monitor portion size carefully to manage postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes elevated total fat (15.4 g/100 g) and saturated fat (3.33 g/100 g); trans fat at 0.11 g/100 g and high sodium (948 mg/100 g) collectively raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of this dish; the combination of high sodium, saturated fat, trans fat, and deep-fried preparation is unfavourable for cardiac health, and grilled or baked alternatives are strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; high sodium (948 mg/100 g) exacerbates tissue oedema associated with RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations; choose baked or grilled chicken and request reduced-sodium preparation to minimise inflammation and oedema.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Fried Chicken is a popular deep-fried chicken dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and Western-style fast-food stalls. The chicken — typically a boneless cutlet or bone-in piece — is coated in a seasoned batter or breading made from wheat flour and cornstarch, then deep-fried in hot oil until the exterior is golden-brown and shatteringly crisp while the interior remains juicy and tender. It is commonly served with honey mustard sauce and a choice of sides such as coleslaw, fries, or rice. Per 100 g, the dish provides 274 kcal, 20.2 g protein, 15.4 g fat (including 3.33 g saturated fat and 0.11 g trans fat), and 13.7 g carbohydrate with 1.4 g dietary fibre. Sodium content is notably high at 948 mg/100 g, making it a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The refined flour coating gives the dish a high estimated glycemic index (~70), which is clinically relevant for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is a meaningful source of protein and provides moderate amounts of potassium (333 mg/100 g), magnesium (33 mg/100 g), and iron (0.69 mg/100 g). It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains gluten allergens from the wheat flour coating. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at Western food stalls and is a staple comfort food across all ethnic communities.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy chicken?
Is fried chicken high in sodium?
What is the GI of fried chicken?
Is crispy chicken suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 for an exact match to breaded fried chicken. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171477). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for breaded fried chicken, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names are present for a Singapore-context dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific Singapore-style crispy fried chicken preparation is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this dish. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish entry; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy consulted but values not directly transferable due to preparation differences. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 by HPB source; this is plausible for an unsweetened savoury breaded chicken preparation with no added sugar in the coating — confirmed zero per HPB data. The input record contained health_suitability notes referencing coconut milk, sambal, anchovies, and curry sauce which are inconsistent with the dish description of crispy fried chicken with honey mustard sauce; these ingredient references have been disregarded as erroneous carry-over from a different dish template. Portions from the input included a 550 g and 450 g plate option referencing Nasi Lemak/Curry Rice accompaniments — these are inconsistent with the dish identity and have been replaced with the 150 g single-piece serving as the canonical default. GI estimated at 70 (high) using analogous breaded fried chicken entries from University of Sydney GI Database (https://www.glycemicindex.com/); glycemic load calculated per 100 g reference portion as (70 × 13.7) / 100 = 9.6. Halal status marked true as chicken is the primary protein and no pork-derived ingredients are indicated; this should be verified against the specific vendor's halal certification. Nutrient source: HPB Singapore Foodo is the primary approved source for core macros; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 171477) is the secondary approved source used for cross-validation of saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C only. No forbidden sources (delivery platforms, calorie-tracking apps, recipe websites) have been used as primary evidence.

Iteration 3: Resolved Q4 failure by ensuring gi_source.organization is explicitly 'University of Sydney GI Database' with no non-approved journal or secondary organization cited in the organization field. Analogous estimation basis retained and fully documented in source_match_notes.
